svg.text: FRCS Part 1 (General Surgery) · UK practice · a descent through the foregut: hiatus → oesophagus → stomach & duodenum
GOJ in place; fundus herniates alongside the oesophagus
sliding + para-oesophageal (GOJ up AND fundus up)
additional organ in the sac (colon, spleen, pancreas)
most sliding = medical; para-oesophageal = consider repair
gastric volvulus (organo-axial — see Gastric volvulus poster), incarceration / strangulation / obstruction, Cameron erosions →
iron-deficiency anaemia & bleeding, respiratory compromise, reflux (I / III)
treat reflux medically; surgery only for refractory disease
repair if symptomatic; asymptomatic large PEH — watchful waiting vs elective repair, individualise (SAGES)
obstruction / volvulus / strangulation / bleeding → emergency surgery
reduce contents, excise the sac, crural repair ± mesh, fundoplication (Nissen / Toupet) ± gastropexy; usually laparoscopic
dysphagia to solids AND liquids, regurgitation, chest
gold standard — elevated IRP with 100% failed
peroral endoscopic myotomy — especially Type III /
nitrates / CCB — adjunct only, poor efficacy
high lesser curve, near the GOJ
duodenal commonest overall (D1; ~90% H. pylori; high
H. pylori, NSAIDs / aspirin, smoking, gastrinoma (ZE),
DU: relieved by food, worse at night; GU: worse with
Upper GI bleeding poster — Forrest / Rockall)
anterior DU; peritonitis, air under diaphragm → omental
always biopsy a gastric ulcer + re-scope to confirm
medical first — surgery for complications or (historically) refractory disease
H. pylori eradication, PPI, stop NSAIDs; repeat OGD for gastric ulcers
perforated DU → omental (Graham) patch + washout; perforated GU → excise / biopsy + patch; bleeding → endoscopy first, then
truncal vagotomy + drainage (pyloroplasty / gastrojejunostomy); highly selective (parietal-cell) vagotomy; antrectomy;
Type I → distal gastrectomy + Billroth I; II / III → vagotomy + antrectomy; IV → Csendes / Pauchet
A para-oesophageal hernia can present as acute gastric volvulus (Borchardt’s triad) — a surgical emergency, not “just a hiatus hernia”.
Iron-deficiency anaemia + a hiatus hernia → think Cameron erosions.
Dysphagia to solids AND liquids from the outset suggests a motility disorder (achalasia); solids-then-liquids suggests a mechanical /
Always exclude pseudoachalasia (GOJ cancer) endoscopically before treating achalasia — especially older patients, short history, weight
Biopsy every gastric ulcer and re-scope to confirm healing — a benign-looking ulcer can be malignant.
Perforated peptic ulcer: free air is absent on erect CXR in up to ~30% — get a CT if suspicion is high.
Sources: SAGES para-oesophageal hernia guidelines · Chicago Classification v4.0 (Yadlapati / Khan et al., 2021) · modified Johnson classification (Johnson, Ann Surg 1965) ·
NICE CG184 & BSG. Forrest / Rockall — see Upper GI bleeding poster; gastric volvulus — see its own poster.
Elective acid-reducing operations are largely historical (PPIs + H. pylori eradication) but remain examinable. IRP = integrated relaxation pressure; POEM = peroral
Revision aid — verify against current local guidance before clinical use.
